questions.question: Why does Haystack store many photos in a single large flat file (volume) instead of one file per photo?
options: Traditional filesystems require one disk seek per file metadata read (inode lookup), so storing 4 billion photos as individual files would make the inode table the bottleneck. Packing thousands of photos into one volume replaces per-file metadata with an in-memory directory.
Flat files are easier to back up because a single tar archive covers all photos.
Operating systems limit the number of files per directory to a few thousand, which would be exceeded quickly.
One file per photo would require more network round-trips to fetch from CDN.
questions.explanation: At 4 billion photos, per-file inode overhead dominates. Haystack eliminates this by using an append-only volume with an in-memory directory mapping photo_id → (volume_id, offset, size) — one seek directly to the data.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of content-addressed storage (like Everstore) over path-based storage for a social media platform?
options: Identical photos uploaded by different users are stored only once automatically — the write path hashes the content and returns the existing URL if the hash already exists, eliminating duplicate storage without a separate deduplication service.
Content-addressed storage is faster to write because it skips checksum validation.
Content-addressed storage eliminates the need for a CDN because URLs are globally unique.
Content-addressed storage makes it easier to delete photos because the hash is the primary key.
questions.explanation: When two users upload the same viral photo or meme, content-addressing stores it once. The write path computes SHA-256, checks if it exists, and returns the existing URL. This is deduplication for free.

questions.question: A Reels video is uploaded at 09:00 and goes viral by 09:05, driving 10 million views in 10 minutes. The CDN edge caches were cold. What is the correct sequence of events that prevents the origin from being overwhelmed?
options: Early engagement signals (likes, shares) trigger the CDN proactive fill system, which pushes the video segments to edge PoPs. Subsequent viewers hit the edge cache; only the first few hundred viewers before the fill completes reach the origin.
The CDN automatically replicates every video to every edge PoP immediately after upload, so the origin is never reached.
The origin auto-scales to 10× capacity within 30 seconds, absorbing all cache-miss traffic without CDN fill.
The video is served directly from Kafka consumer offset storage during viral spikes to bypass the origin.
questions.explanation: Proactive fill uses engagement prediction: when early signals (share rate, repost velocity) exceed a threshold, a fill job pushes the video segments to all relevant edge PoPs. This race between viral spread and fill propagation is the core challenge.

questions.question: Why does Meta transcode Reels videos to AV1 in addition to H.264 and VP9, given that AV1 encoding costs significantly more compute?
options: AV1 achieves ~50% smaller file size at equivalent visual quality compared to H.264. At 140 billion plays per day, a 50% reduction in CDN bandwidth and storage cost dwarfs the one-time encoding cost.
AV1 is the only codec supported by Meta's CDN infrastructure.
AV1 allows videos to be stored without encryption, reducing overhead.
AV1 encoding is faster than H.264 for videos longer than 60 seconds.
questions.explanation: Encoding is a one-time cost. Serving happens billions of times per day. Halving the bits per view at 140B plays/day saves enormous CDN and storage costs. This is the correct trade-off reasoning.

questions.question: What happens to Haystack needles when a user deletes a photo?
options: The needle's delete flag is set in the in-memory directory and on disk. The bytes are not immediately reclaimed. A background compaction job periodically rewrites volumes, skipping deleted needles, to reclaim space.
The needle bytes are immediately overwritten with zeros to reclaim space and ensure the photo cannot be recovered.
The volume file containing the needle is deleted and rebuilt from scratch without the deleted photo.
Deleted photos are moved to a separate cold-tier volume for regulatory hold before permanent deletion.
questions.explanation: Haystack is append-only. Deletion sets a flag. Compaction is the offline process that rewrites volumes, dropping deleted needles. This avoids random I/O at delete time and simplifies write path consistency.

questions.question: A transcoding worker crashes after downloading the original video but before finishing all 15 variants. How does the pipeline recover without re-encoding variants that already completed?
options: Transcoding jobs track per-variant completion status. On retry, the worker checks which variants already exist in Everstore (by content-addressed hash) and skips those, only re-encoding missing variants.
The worker restarts the entire job from scratch because partial transcoding states cannot be trusted.
A supervisor process kills all incomplete variants and forces a clean re-run from the original.
The Kafka offset is not committed until all variants complete, so the next worker picks up the job and re-runs everything.
questions.explanation: Idempotency through content-addressed storage: a completed variant has a deterministic hash. The retry path checks existence before re-encoding. This makes the pipeline resilient to worker failures without duplicating work.

questions.question: Why does Meta run both DASH and HLS manifests for the same Reels video?
options: DASH is used for Android and web clients where Meta controls the player; HLS is required for iOS and Safari due to Apple's platform requirement that video streaming use HLS.
DASH and HLS use different segment sizes, and serving both allows clients to choose the more efficient one for their bandwidth.
DASH is used for short videos under 60 seconds; HLS is used for longer content.
HLS is the primary format; DASH is a fallback used only when HLS decoding fails on Android.
questions.explanation: Apple mandates HLS for iOS video playback. DASH is the open standard used on Android and web. Supporting both maximizes device coverage without maintaining separate video stores — both manifests reference the same underlying segment files.

questions.question: A photo uploaded 3 years ago is stored in cold tier. A user opens their old album and views it. What is the correct behavior and the latency implication?
options: The CDN cache miss propagates to the origin, which fetches from cold tier storage. Cold tier retrieval adds seconds of latency (re-warm step). The photo is promoted back to warm tier after access to reduce latency on subsequent views.
Cold tier photos return a 404 because they are no longer in active storage.
Cold tier photos are served instantly because the cold tier uses the same SSD infrastructure as hot tier, just with lower replication.
The user receives a notification that the photo requires 24 hours to retrieve from archival storage.
questions.explanation: Cold tier retrieval is not instant — it involves erasure code reconstruction or off-site fetch. The system can serve it but with higher latency. Promoting frequently re-accessed cold objects back to warm tier is the correct optimization.
